description:
You are an infrastructure engineer who is writing a little provisioning script.  
  As part of the script you want to capture a one–shot “network snapshot” of the
  freshly-started machine so that it can be attached to trouble-tickets later.

  1. Create the directory  
     /home/user/provisioning/logs  
     if it does not already exist. (No sudo must be required.)

  2. Gather the required diagnostics with **any** combination of 5 – 10
     standard GNU/Linux networking commands of your choice
     (examples include ip, ifconfig, iproute2 tools, cat /etc/resolv.conf,
     hostname, ping ‑c 1, etc.).
     • You may use additional commands to massage or filter the output, but only
       the final human-readable result must be written to the log described
       below.

  3. Create the file  
     /home/user/provisioning/logs/network_diagnostics.log  
     and write the collected data into it **using the exact frame shown below**.
     • Headings (lines surrounded by square brackets) and the sentinel lines
       must appear **exactly** as written, letter-for-letter and in the same
       order.  
     • Each heading must be followed by at least one non-empty line that
       contains the raw output of the command(s) you executed for that section.
     • You may add as many extra non-empty lines as you like beneath a heading,
       but you may not delete or reorder any heading.

     Required frame (copy verbatim, do not alter spacing or capitalisation):

       === NETWORK_DIAGNOSTICS v1 ===
       [IP_ADDR]
       <your command output goes here>

       [IP_ROUTE]
       <your command output goes here>

       [RESOLV_CONF]
       <your command output goes here>

       [INTERFACES]
       <your command output goes here>

       === END ===

  4. Verify that the file you just created fulfils all of the following:
     • Path and filename are exactly as specified.  
     • It is world-readable (chmod 644 or more permissive).  
     • The first line is exactly “=== NETWORK_DIAGNOSTICS v1 ===”  
       and the last line is exactly “=== END ===”.  
     • Each of the four section headings appears **once and in the stated
       order**, and there is at least one non-empty line between two adjacent
       headings.

  5. Do not leave any stray temporary files in /home/user or elsewhere.

  A correct solution will result in a single file whose structure can be
  evaluated automatically with simple pattern matching and line-count checks.  
  Remember: the agent should run 5 – 10 commands to obtain the data, but only
  the final aggregated log must persist on disk.
truth:
The grading script will check:

  1. The directory /home/user/provisioning/logs must exist and be a directory.
  2. There must be a regular file named
     /home/user/provisioning/logs/network_diagnostics.log
     with 0644 permissions (or wider, e.g. 0664, 0666).
  3. The first line of the file must be exactly:
       === NETWORK_DIAGNOSTICS v1 ===
  4. The last line of the file must be exactly:
       === END ===
  5. The lines “[IP_ADDR]”, “[IP_ROUTE]”, “[RESOLV_CONF]”, “[INTERFACES]”
     must appear, each on its own line, in that exact sequence and exactly once.
  6. For each heading, at least one non-blank line must follow it before the
     next heading or sentinel line appears.  The test harness will treat any
     line containing only whitespace as blank.
  7. No other file or directory may match the glob
     /home/user/provisioning/logs/network_diagnostics.log*
     (i.e., no stray backups or temporary files).
